coredata.dc:title: A continuous supply chain management approach using SPJS-Fuzzy DEMATEL and LPWBN for automotive electric vehicles in India

coredata.dc:creator: Rohit, Kartik
Verma, Ajay
Dhairiyasamy, Ratchagaraja
Gabiriel, Deepika
coredata.dc:description: The rapid adoption of Electric Vehicles (EVs) is essential for reducing greenhouse gas emissions and promoting sustainable transportation. In India, the EV market faces significant challenges in supply chain management (SCM), particularly in integrating third-party recyclers to handle EV components and lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) efficiently. Existing studies lead to inefficiencies and potential environmental hazards. Addressing this critical gap, we propose a continuous SCM framework tailored for automotive EVs in India, incorporating EV component recyclers using Shannon's Probabilistic Jaccard Similarity-based Fuzzy Decision Making Trial and Evaluation Laboratory (SPJS-Fuzzy DEMATEL) and Lagrange Interpolation-based Probability Weighting Bayesian Network (LPWBN). The applicability of our method lies in its ability to enhance transparency, optimize resource utilization, and mitigate risks in the EV supply chain. Starting with the collection and transportation of raw materials to Indian manufacturing units, our framework employs JS-Fuzzy logic with sensor data to prevent manufacturing disruptions. The LPWBN enhances transparency between manufacturers and consumers, facilitating better demand forecasting and supply planning. Damaged or end-of-life products are routed to third-party recyclers, where JS-Fuzzy logic ensures the quality of recycled resources for remanufacturing. By clustering enablers and analyzing their correlations using SPJS-Fuzzy DEMATEL, we assess the impacts of economic, cultural, organizational, social, and environmental aspects on SCM. India, with its burgeoning EV market and unique socio-economic conditions, serves as an ideal context for applying our framework. Experimental results demonstrate that our proposed system achieves a 99.74 % reliability score, outperforming existing methods and highlighting its potential to advance sustainable EV supply chains in India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The rapid adoption of Electric Vehicles (EVs) is essential for reducing greenhouse gas emissions and promoting sustainable transportation. In India, the EV market faces significant challenges in supply chain management (SCM), particularly in integrating third-party recyclers to handle EV components and lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) efficiently. Existing studies lead to inefficiencies and potential environmental hazards. Addressing this critical gap, we propose a continuous SCM framework tailored for automotive EVs in India, incorporating EV component recyclers using Shannon's Probabilistic Jaccard Similarity-based Fuzzy Decision Making Trial and Evaluation Laboratory (SPJS-Fuzzy DEMATEL) and Lagrange Interpolation-based Probability Weighting Bayesian Network (LPWBN). The applicability of our method lies in its ability to enhance transparency, optimize resource utilization, and mitigate risks in the EV supply chain. Starting with the collection and transportation of raw materials to Indian manufacturing units, our framework employs JS-Fuzzy logic with sensor data to prevent manufacturing disruptions. The LPWBN enhances transparency between manufacturers and consumers, facilitating better demand forecasting and supply planning. Damaged or end-of-life products are routed to third-party recyclers, where JS-Fuzzy logic ensures the quality of recycled resources for remanufacturing. By clustering enablers and analyzing their correlations using SPJS-Fuzzy DEMATEL, we assess the impacts of economic, cultural, organizational, social, and environmental aspects on SCM. India, with its burgeoning EV market and unique socio-economic conditions, serves as an ideal context for applying our framework. Experimental results demonstrate that our proposed system achieves a 99.74 % reliability score, outperforming existing methods and highlighting its potential to advance sustainable EV supply chains in India.

ce:section.ce:para: An electric motor taking energy from inbuilt power batteries and external power sources can power an EV [1]. EVs, which are cost-efficient and eco-friendly, are six times more effective than conventional combustion engine vehicles, which create more pollution in Indian cities. The Indian government largely supports EV adoption, and private automobile industries worked to create innovative EVs that are suitable for India's roads and traffic conditions [2]. SCM plays a vital role in product manufacturing in the automobile industry. A network with a combination of people, manufacturing, raw materials, and logistics that helps in product manufacturing and marketing is known as SCM [3]. Closed-Loop Supply Chain (CLSC), Sustainable Supply Chain Management (SSCM), and Green Supply Chain Management (GSCM) are the types of SCM [4]. EV's supply chain includes the extraction and refining of raw materials, manufacturing of LIBs, dealerships, delivery, and recycling of EV components. The SCM for EVs is an important task since it gains both the customer and the supplier [5]. The exponential growth of the electric vehicle (EV) market globally, particularly in India, has underscored the critical importance of effective supply chain management (SCM). India's commitment to reducing carbon emissions and dependence on fossil fuels has accelerated the adoption of EVs. However, this rapid adoption brings forth challenges such as supply chain disruptions, inadequate infrastructure, and inefficiencies in managing the flow of materials and information [6]. Addressing these challenges is imperative to ensure the sustainability and reliability of the EV industry in India.

In the SCM, manufacturing and recycling EV batteries have an equal and essential role in ensuring the sustainability, reliability, and efficiency of the EV supply chain [7]. An elevating amount of EV lithium-ion battery usage causes emergent waste management challenges. EV batteries retire when their capacity lowers to 70–80 % of the initial capacity [8]. EV batteries' components are recycled and remanufactured from both new and recycled resources. Owing to its environmental benefits and low consumption of new raw materials, the remanufacturing of LIBs from recycled waste batteries is progressively gaining appreciation from several EV markets in India as well as other governments [9]. The disruption of raw materials during the manufacturing and remanufacturing process is caused by ineffective SCM planning [10]. The organizational environment may become volatile, leaving businesses extremely unstable and facing huge losses in a short time during unavoidable and pandemic situations [11]. During these disruptive events, all enterprises are struggling with their survival plans [12]. These are all handled by efficiently managing the raw materials for manufacturing and measuring the raw materials quantity in the warehouse. Hence, to fulfill the consumer's demands in the sudden pandemic and other situations, an important SCM plan is implemented within the organization [13]. Several existing research works concentrated on maintaining efficient SCM for EVs by utilizing the Multi-Layer Bayesian Network, DEMATEL, and Reuse, Remanufacture, and Recycle techniques. Nevertheless, risks linked to uncertainties in demand and supply are increased by the lack of transparency within SCM [14]. Thus, this framework proposes a continuous SCM using SPJS-FuzzyDEMATEL and LPWBN on automotive EVs.

This study aims to fill the identified research gaps by developing a continuous SCM framework that integrates comprehensive enablers and employs advanced analytical methods. Focusing on the Indian EV industry, the proposed framework has the potential to enhance supply chain efficiency, sustainability, and resilience. The findings can inform policymakers, industry practitioners, and researchers interested in SCM optimization in emerging markets. The study of Electric Vehicle (EV) supply chains has garnered significant academic attention; however, notable gaps persist in the existing literature. One of the primary gaps is the insufficient consideration of cultural and organizational factors that influence supply chain management (SCM), particularly in the Indian context. Current research predominantly emphasizes economic and environmental dimensions, overlooking the socio-organizational dynamics that are integral to the efficiency and adaptability of supply chains [15]. Furthermore, the role of third-party recyclers, which is critical to advancing circular economy principles and sustainable practices, is often excluded from prevailing frameworks [16]. This omission limits the scope for developing a comprehensive and sustainable approach to SCM. Additionally, traditional analytical methods, such as basic DEMATEL and Bayesian Networks, have limitations in addressing uncertainties and capturing the intricate interrelationships among supply chain enablers [17]. Addressing these gaps is imperative for enhancing the effectiveness and sustainability of SCM within the Indian EV sector. Incorporating cultural and organizational factors into the analysis provides a holistic understanding of the supply chain, enabling the design of strategies that are more aligned with the specific requirements of the Indian market. Including third-party recyclers within SCM frameworks supports the development of environmentally sustainable and resource-efficient supply chain practices. Moreover, the application of advanced analytical methods improves the capacity to address and understand complex interdependencies, thereby facilitating more informed and reliable decision-making processes.

Advanced analytical tools, such as SPJS-Fuzzy DEMATEL and the Lagrange interpolation-based Probability Weighting Bayesian Network (LPWBN), are proposed to address the identified gaps. The SPJS-Fuzzy DEMATEL method integrates Shannon's Probabilistic Jaccard Similarity into the traditional Fuzzy DEMATEL framework, effectively addressing its limitations in analyzing overlapping causal relationships and managing uncertainties among supply chain enablers. In recent years, several advanced fuzzy sets, such as Neutrosophic sets, type-2 neutrosophic sets, rough numbers, and Z-numbers, have been utilized to address uncertainties and complexities in decision-making frameworks. Neutrosophic sets and type-2 neutrosophic sets provide enhanced flexibility by incorporating degrees of truth, falsity, and indeterminacy, making them particularly suitable for highly subjective environments. Rough numbers excel in managing granularity and approximations in data [2], while Z-numbers introduce an additional reliability component to handle uncertainty in probability distributions. Despite their robustness, these methods are computationally intensive and require intricate modeling that may not align with the data availability and operational requirements in emerging markets like India. In contrast, the Similarity-based Fuzzy approach integrates the simplicity of fuzzy logic with probabilistic measures, allowing for a balance between computational efficiency and the precision needed to address overlapping causal relationships and uncertainties. This approach not only reduces the computational overhead but also enhances the interpretability of results, making it particularly suitable for dynamic supply chain scenarios like those in the Indian EV sector. Furthermore, the literature demonstrates its successful application in similar contexts, such as healthcare logistics and sustainable manufacturing [12,18], thereby justifying its preference for the proposed framework.

This enhanced approach allows for more precise analysis by incorporating probabilistic measures and fuzzy logic. Similarly, the LPWBN mitigates the shortcomings of conventional Bayesian Networks, such as reliance on static prior probabilities, by employing interpolation techniques for probability weighting. This refinement enhances the transparency and reliability of network analyses in supply chain scenarios. The application of these integrated methods to the Indian EV supply chain context offers significant benefits. The SPJS-Fuzzy DEMATEL framework provides a deeper understanding of the interdependencies among economic, cultural, organizational, social, and environmental factors, facilitating the development of well-informed strategies. Concurrently, the LPWBN enhances transparency and communication between supply chain stakeholders, such as manufacturers and consumers, by addressing uncertainties related to demand and supply dynamics. These methodologies collectively contribute to the establishment of a more resilient, transparent, and sustainable supply chain tailored to the complexities of the Indian EV sector.

The prevailing works on electric vehicle (EV) supply chain management (SCM) exhibit significant limitations. Most existing studies have focused predominantly on the economic, social, and environmental dimensions of the automotive EV supply chain while neglecting the equally critical cultural and organizational aspects, which are key to SCM efficiency and adaptability. For instance, the closed-loop supply chain (CLSC) model presented by Ma [19] primarily addressed a single manufacturer and consumer scenario but failed to incorporate third-party recyclers, resulting in supply chain inefficiencies. Similarly, researchers examined stock-out issues during disruptions or pandemics caused by reliance on Just-In-Time (JIT) production systems. Ayati et al. [17] investigated barriers to remanufacturing but did not adequately assess the impact of these barriers on the implementation of remanufacturing processes. Additionally, a lack of transparency in many existing SCM frameworks has hindered effective communication between stakeholders, escalating the risks associated with demand and supply uncertainties.

This study aims to address the identified limitations of existing works through the development of a comprehensive SCM framework. The proposed approach seeks to integrate five key aspects: economic, cultural, organizational, social, and environmental. It introduces an EV component recycler to promote circular economy principles. To mitigate stock-out risks during pandemics or disruptions, the framework employs JS-Fuzzy logic. Furthermore, it leverages SPJS-DEMATEL to analyze the impact of various barriers within the SCM. Lastly, the lack of transparency in traditional models is tackled using a Lagrange Probability Weighting Bayesian Network (LPWBN), enhancing communication and reducing uncertainties.

Given the limitations in existing EV supply chain management studies, particularly the inadequate focus on cultural and organizational factors and the exclusion of third-party recyclers, this research seeks to explore the following question: How can a continuous supply chain management framework for automotive electric vehicles in India be developed that effectively integrates economic, cultural, organizational, social, and environmental aspects; includes third-party recyclers; enhances transparency between manufacturers and consumers; and mitigates risks associated with supply and demand uncertainties?

Ma (2022) developed a mathematical model to analyze demand disruptions and government subsidies in closed-loop SCM, finding that subsidizing retailers increased recovery rates and profits. However, the absence of third-party recyclers limited the model's efficiency, highlighting the need for a more inclusive approach. Hosseini and Ivanov proposed a disruption model using a multi-layer Bayesian Network to quantify supply chain disruptions during COVID-19. While effective in decision support, reliance on JIT systems caused stock-out issues, indicating the need for resilient inventory management strategies.Ayati et al. [17] examined barriers in circular supply chains using 3R recovery techniques, categorizing barriers into seven groups. While consumer willingness to purchase recovered products increased, the model lacked a detailed analysis of barriers impacting remanufacturing processes, necessitating further exploration.Gupta et al. [15] identified barriers to EV adoption in India using structural modeling and best-worst methods, emphasizing the influence of policy gaps and low awareness. However, the model's sensitivity to barrier variability requires adaptive frameworks for dynamic conditions.Tripathy et al. [20] used the Delphi method to explore SCM enablers for EV battery recycling, providing insights for policy prioritization. The method's reliance on participant rationality introduced biases, underscoring the need for improved data collection techniques.Tarei et al. [21] proposed a hybrid decision-making model to prioritize barriers to EV adoption. While effective in identifying interrelationships, the model requires validation for large-scale generalization, emphasizing the need for broader testing.Hu et al. [22] designed a sustainable recycling network for EV batteries using 3R strategies and cost optimization. Although effective in minimizing costs and emissions, the model lacked dynamic recycling cycle considerations, limiting resource utilization.Mu et al. [23] introduced a dynamic reverse logistics network for retired EV batteries, outperforming static models in optimization. However, uncertain parameters reduced its reliability, indicating a need for robust uncertainty management.Feng et al. [24] developed a scalable logistics network for closed-loop operations under uncertainty, enhancing logistics efficiency. However, its applicability in complex environments remains limited, requiring further refinement.Wang et al. [25] employed evolutionary game theory to analyze reverse SCM under government supervision. The study revealed the importance of collaboration but did not assess carbon taxes' impact, suggesting the need for more comprehensive evaluation.

The existing literature provides significant knowledge on various aspects of supply chain management, circular economy, and electric vehicle adoption. However, limitations such as the exclusion of key stakeholders, insufficient focus on dynamic and uncertain conditions, and reliance on static models indicate the need for more advanced and comprehensive approaches. Employing integrative frameworks and analytical methods can substantially enhance the resilience, efficiency, and sustainability of EV supply chains.

Cultural and organizational factors play a crucial role in shaping the effectiveness of supply chain management (SCM), especially in the context of electric vehicles (EVs). Cultural dimensions such as societal values, consumer behavior, local customs, and attitudes towards technology adoption significantly influence the success of SCM strategies [26]. In India, the diversity of cultural practices directly impacts how EVs are perceived and accepted, highlighting the importance of aligning supply chain strategies with local cultural nuances to enhance market penetration.Organizational factors, including leadership approaches, management practices, internal communication, and corporate culture, are equally vital in determining the success of SCM processes. Organizations fostering a culture of innovation, adaptability, and collaboration demonstrate greater resilience in managing supply chain disruptions and adopting advanced technologies [18]. The alignment of organizational practices with SCM strategies is crucial for enhancing operational efficiency and meeting evolving market demands.

Despite their significance, these factors are often underexplored in existing research. Studies such as Ma (2022) and Hosseini and Ivanov [27] focus predominantly on mathematical modeling and disruption analysis, with limited attention to the role of cultural attitudes in shaping supply chain dynamics. Similarly, research by Ayati et al. [17] and Tripathy et al. [20] examines barriers to remanufacturing and recycling but neglects the influence of organizational structures and cultures on these processes. This gap in the literature underscores the need to integrate cultural and organizational factors into SCM models to achieve a more comprehensive understanding of supply chain challenges [6]. Incorporating cultural and organizational factors into SCM frameworks is essential to developing effective and sustainable strategies. Addressing cultural attitudes can enhance consumer acceptance of EVs, while identifying and mitigating organizational barriers can improve operational efficiency and promote innovation. Neglecting these dimensions risks creating incomplete models that fail to capture the complexities of SCM in diverse and dynamic markets like India.

The research on supply chain management (SCM) and electric vehicle (EV) adoption in India, outlines challenges like high logistics costs, fragmented supply chains, infrastructural gaps, and reliance on imports as shown in Table 1 . Strategies include aligning SCM with business goals, adopting green practices, and leveraging IoT, AI, and blockchain technologies. These measures enhance collaboration, reduce inventories, boost efficiency, and promote sustainability via eco-design and renewable energy. India's EV market is projected to reach USD 47 billion by 2026, with reduced emissions. However, regulatory gaps, infrastructure deficits, and reliance on imported lithium-ion batteries demand policy reforms and investments to ensure progress.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Proposed SCM methodology for automotive EV using SPJS-Fuzzy DEMATEL and LPWBN
ce:section.ce:para: The SCM with component recyclers for automotive Indian EVs using SPJS-FuzzyDEMATEL and LPWBN is proposed. The raw materials for EVs are gathered and processed in the manufacturing unit to reach the consumers with the final product, along with risk level analysis of different aspects. Fig. 1 exhibits the proposed SCM framework of the EV.

The framework is started by extracting the raw material and transporting it to the manufacturing unit's warehouse in India. Next, to avoid manufacturing distortion, the warehouse quantity is optimized by utilizing JS-Fuzzy. In the components manufacturing unit, the raw materials are processed and the final product EV is acquired. Then, the LPWBN enhances the transparency between the manufacturer and the consumer. After that, to overcome the disruptions during pandemic conditions, the defective products are recycled and checked. Next, the barriers are examined to alert the manufacturing unit about the impact of different aspects of the SCM. The proposed framework's process is briefly described further.

The SCM is regarded as a network with a combination of people, raw materials, manufacturing, and transportation, which help in product manufacturing and marketing.The SCM of EVs in India includes the collection of raw materials, transportation, manufacturing, consumers, and recyclers. Primarily, the raw materials for the EVs, such as cobalt, lithium, manganese, graphite, steel, and nickel are gathered and transported to the manufacturing unit in India.

The raw materials are processed in the manufacturing unit, and they are converted into the final product. Sensors are used to measure the raw material quantity using JS-Fuzzy to avoid the disruption of raw materials during manufacturing. The Fuzzy logic system is used because of its robustness without the requirement for any precise inputs. However, it produces many solutions that make it unreliable. Thus, to get effective results, the Jaccard Similarity membership function is utilized in the fuzzy logic system. Hence, the process of JS-Fuzzy is explained below.

a. Rule-base: T he warehouse's optimal condition is set as the threshold value under if-then conditions. The optimal raw material quantity

Then, the EV manufacturing is processed; thus, the output obtained from the component manufacturing unit is the final product, which is an EV. Next, the final product is ready to reach the consumers. The Bayesian Network (BN) construction provides transparency between the manufacturer and the consumer. The enablers' data are considered as the input, and a belief network structure is produced. Still, the selection of the prior probability takes the previous information or presumptions, which may affect the outcomes. Thus, to overcome this issue, Lagrange interpolation in the Probability Weighting function is utilized instead of prior probability in BN construction. in the manufacturing unit is significantly provided to the consumers. Then, to minimize greenhouse gas emissions, eliminate waste, and effectively utilize resources to enhance the circular economy, the damaged or returned products from the consumers are given to the EV component recycler. Next, the recycled resources are sent to the remanufacturing unit.To overcome the disruptions in manufacturing during pandemic conditions, the quantity of resources is also measured using the JS-Fuzzy. LPWBN provides transparency between the remanufacturing unit and the consumer. Then, the final product reaches the consumer, and the default product is recycled, which continues in the loop.

The proposed supply chain management (SCM) framework integrates cultural and organizational dimensions to ensure a comprehensive and holistic approach. It emphasizes the importance of regional consumer behaviors, societal norms, and cultural attitudes toward the adoption of electric vehicle (EV). By leveraging consumer data, marketing strategies are customized to overcome cultural barriers and improve public acceptance of EV technologies. Internal processes within manufacturing units and among supply chain partners are evaluated to promote collaboration and innovation. Training programs and organizational development initiatives are designed to align employee mindsets and practices with the company's sustainability objectives.

Cultural and organizational data are analyzed using the KauForgyman-Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers (KaF-BERT) model. This advanced natural language processing tool extracts nuanced insights from textual data related to cultural attitudes and organizational practices. The HTC-KMeans clustering algorithm is employed to group cultural and organizational enablers with economic, social, and environmental factors. This approach facilitates the development of targeted strategies by addressing specific clusters of interconnected enablers. The SPJS-Fuzzy DEMATEL method is utilized to evaluate the influence of cultural and organizational factors on the supply chain. For instance, resistance to change within organizations may causally impact the adoption of innovative recycling processes. This integration enhances the adaptability and efficiency of the SCM framework, ensuring alignment with sustainability goals and regional dynamics. To understand the impact of the different enablers, such as economic, cultural, organizational, social, and environmental aspects, in SCM of Indian EVs, the cause and effects of the enablers

These data are transformed by utilizing the BERT algorithm, which is an easily and efficiently defined natural language processing model and can be trained on a large corpus. The BERT can read the text in both directions. To train the BERT models, the weights are created randomly from a normal distribution, which is consistently worse. Thus, it can result in a decrease in the performance of the training process. Hence, the KauForgyman-based weights will be assigned in the BERT algorithm. The KaF-BERT process is explained further. Primarily, by using Mask Language Modeling (MLM) and Next Sentence Prediction (NSP), KaF-BERT is trained on a large corpus of text data. Lastly, based on SPJS-Fuzzy DEMATEL, the cause and effect analysis of the clustered enablers
(
Φ
)
is carried out. By using Fuzzy DEMATEL, the composition of the system elements is reduced, and the relationship between elements is simplified. Thus, it is used to examine the interrelationships among the enablers by finding the cause-and-effect behavior. However, there is the possibility of overlapping causal relationships, thus leading to less precise outcomes. Hence, to overcome this issue, Shannon's probabilistic entropy measure is added to the pairwise matrix between the enablers, and the conventional fuzzy membership is replaced with the Jaccard Similarity membership. Therefore, the process is named as SPJS-Fuzzy DEMATEL, which is explained below:

The above analysis considers the obtained higher variance as the higher risk value of SCM. The SCM is further improved to enhance its performance efficacy. The proposed model's performance is discussed further.

By comparing the proposed model's performance with that of existing models, the proposed system's efficacy is proved. The experimental evaluation is done by implementing it in the MATLAB working platform.

From the publicly available data sources mentioned in the reference section, the NAATBatt Lithium-Ion Battery Supply Chain Database (NLIBSCD) and Stakeholder dataset are used. The NLIBSCD contains 99 LIBs' supply chain data. Among all, 80 % of the data is employed for training, and 20 % of the data is utilized for testing. The Stakeholder dataset contains 94 stakeholder data in the electrical and electronic equipment value chain. From this, 80 % of the data is taken for training, and the remaining 20 % is used for testing.

In Table, the dataset description of the NLIBSCD and Stakeholder dataset, which are used for the proposed framework's experimental evaluation, is described. The hardware configuration comprised an Intel ® Core™ i7–10750H CPU operating at 2.60 GHz, equipped with 16 GB DDR4 RAM and a 512 GB SSD. Graphics processing was supported by an NVIDIA® GeForce® GTX 1660 Ti GPU featuring 6 GB GDDR6 VRAM. The software environment consisted of Windows 10 Pro 64-bit as the operating system, with MATLAB R2021b employed as the primary computational platform. Specific toolboxes and libraries included the Fuzzy Logic Toolbox for the implementation of JS-Fuzzy Logic and SPJS-Fuzzy DEMATEL, the Statistics and Machine Learning Toolbox for HTC-KMeans clustering, the Deep Learning Toolbox for KaF-BERT model implementation, and the Bayesian Network Toolbox for constructing the LPWBN.

The implementation of the proposed framework was carried out with detailed attention to each component. For JS-Fuzzy Logic, membership functions were defined using Jaccard Similarity measures, and rule bases were formulated using MATLAB's Fuzzy Logic Designer. The defuzzification process was executed using the centroid method to ensure precise results. The KaF-BERT model was fine-tuned with KauForgyman weight initialization and GPU acceleration during training, utilizing a dataset of textual enablers to facilitate comprehensive semantic analysis. For HTC-KMeans Clustering, centroid initialization was performed using Hotelling's T-cube distribution, with iterations proceeding until convergence was achieved (centroid change < 10⁻⁶). The LPWBN construction employed a Lagrange interpolation-based probability weighting function, and the network structure was developed using the hill-climbing algorithm. The runtime performance demonstrated optimal efficiency, with the entire process completed in two hours and a peak memory utilization of 8 GB during KaF-BERT training. The data availability is given in Supplementary material.
The robustness of the proposed SPJS-Fuzzy DEMATEL is assessed with prevailing models, such as DEMATEL, Fuzzy, Adaptive Neuro-Fuzzy Inference System (ANFIS), and Decision rule.

The table analyzes the performance of the proposed SPJS-FuzzyDEMATEL and prevailing models regarding the Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) as well as comparative fit index metrics. The prevailing DEMATEL's RMSE and comparative fit index are 0.065 and 0.7735, correspondingly, while the proposed model efficiently overcomes the overlapping causal relationship issues in the prevailing model. Thus, when compared to all other prevailing systems, the proposed model had a minimum RMSE of 0.00312 and a maximum comparative fit index of 0.7875. Hence, the analysis demonstrates the significance of the proposed model.

The performance of the proposed HTC-KMeans clustering algorithm is analogized with prevailing algorithms, such as KMeans, Gaussian Mixture Model Clustering (GMMC), Density-Based Clustering (DBC), and WBC.

In Table 4 , the clustering time analysis of the proposed HTC-KMeans and existing clustering algorithms is indicated. The proposed model takes a minimum of 1237 ms to cluster the enablers. But, the prevailing GMMC and DBC take 1905 ms and 2106 ms for clustering. Similarly, the other existing models also take more time to cluster. The proposed obtained minimum clustering time as the result of an effective centroid selection using Hotelling's T-cube distribution function. Hence, this analysis stated the proposed model's superiority.
The performance of the proposed JS-Fuzzy and the existing Gaussian fuzzy, fuzzy logic system, ANFIS, and decision rule are evaluated and analogized.

Fig. 5 indicates the performance evaluation of the proposed JS-Fuzzy and existing models regarding the fuzzification time, defuzzification time, and rule generation time. For fuzzification, defuzzification, and rule generation processes, the existing models took an average of 2936.26 ms, 3252.5 ms, and 3310.5 ms, while the proposed JS-Fuzzy takes only 980 ms, 1100 ms, and 1236 ms, which are considerably lower than the prevailing models. This significant performance of the proposed model is attained by employing the Jaccard Similarity membership function to overcome unreliable solutions with the conventional model.
The performance of the proposed LPWBN is examined and analogized with the prevailing models, such as BN, Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM), Deep Neural Network (DNN), and Artificial Neural Network (ANN).

In Fig. 6 , the performance evaluation of the proposed and existing models based on the performance metrics, such as accuracy, precision, recall, F-measure, False Positive Rate (FPR), False Negative Rate (FNR), Negative Predictive Value (NPV), and Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC) is presented. Accuracy, precision, recall, F-measure, FPR, FNR, NPV, and MCC achieved by the proposed LPWBN are 0.9973, 0.9769, 0.9757, 0.9763, 0.001357, 0.0242214, 0.9985, and 0.9749508, correspondingly. The prevailing ANN had 0.9678 accuracy, 0.7107 precision, 0.7099 recall, 0.71032 F-measure, 0.016996 FPR, 0.2900807 FNR, 0.9829 NPV, and 0.6932988 MCC, which are lower than the proposed model because of prior probability. Likewise, when compared to the proposed system, the other prevailing models also achieved lower performances. This proves the robustness of the proposed LPWBN, thus overcoming the prior probability issue with the Lagrange interpolation-based Probability weighting function. To prove the proposed system's significance in the effective EV-SCM in India, the proposed system's performance is analogized with the related works.

The comparative analysis of the proposed system with prevailing works is indicated in Table 5 . The analysis uses [44–48] works. In the electric vehicles SCM systems, several approaches like Game theory, integration of the Allocation model with distributed energy system optimization model, Pyrometallurgical and Hydrometallurgical recycling technologies, stochastic activity model, and Stackelberg game pricing method are employed. The prevailing models examined the SCM aspects like economic, social, and environmental impacts. Along with that, the prevailing [45] acquired a profit of 1.27 billion with EV Closed-Loop SCM. Likewise, the other prevailing models are also gaining more profits with the SCM techniques they have used. Nevertheless, the proposed model is more efficient since it examines five various aspects of SCM, such as economic, cultural, organizational, social, and environmental, as well as obtains 1.34 billion profits by efficiently balancing the raw material quantity by JS-Fuzzy. Similarly, the transparency between manufacturers and consumers is also improved by the proposed model. Therefore, the comparative evaluation stated that when compared to the prevailing models, the proposed SCM system is more significant.

This framework proposes an effective and continuous SCM model for Indian EVs using the SPJS-Fuzzy DEMATEL and LPWBN. JS-Fuzzy effectively measures and manages the quantity of raw material to reduce disruption during the manufacturing process. Next, the LPWBN increases the transparency of the manufacturing process; the SCM aspects are examined to improve the SCM performances. The proposed model's experimental evaluation is implemented with the NLIBSCD and Stakeholder dataset. As per the experimental outcomes, the proposed model has a 0.9974 reliability score, 0.7875 comparative fit index, and 0.00312 RMSE for the aspect analysis. After that, the proposed model attained 0.9973 accuracy and 0.9985 NPV for transparency improvement. Moreover, the proposed model takes a minimum of 980 ms and 1100 ms for fuzzification and defuzzification. Hence, the proposed framework's overall performance evaluation stated the proposed model's robustness in the continuous SCM of EVs in India. Nevertheless, this work didn't concentrate on data privacy and security during transparency improvement. By explicitly incorporating cultural and organizational factors, the proposed SCM framework provides a more comprehensive approach to managing the EV supply chain in India. Understanding and addressing cultural attitudes toward EVs can enhance consumer acceptance and adoption rates. Simultaneously, fostering an organizational culture that values sustainability and innovation can improve operational efficiency and resilience against disruptions. This holistic integration ensures that the supply chain is robust, efficient, and aligned with the diverse needs of the Indian market. Thus, future work will concentrate on developing blockchain-centric EV supply chains with critical technical characteristics-based data privacy and security models.

Kartik Rohit: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Data curation, Conceptualization. Ajay Verma: Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation. Ratchagaraja Dhairiyasamy: Supervision, Software, Resources, Methodology. Deepika Gabiriel: Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis.
